origin: SE Europe to central Asia (Balkans, Ukraine, steppe belt to Iran)
habitat: Dry stony grasslands, open steppe, and rocky hillsides on lean alkaline soils
blurb: The elegant mullein: slender wispy spires of flat saucer-shaped flowers in shades of purple, rose, and white rise from a bold basal rosette of crinkled leaves — far more refined than the roadside yellow club of common mullein. Short-lived but self-sows reliably on lean, sharply drained ground; a xeric Front Range perennial that bees and pollinators work steadily through early summer.
size: 2–3 ft tall in bloom × 12–18 in wide
water: Low; xeric once established — sharp drainage is essential, wet soil kills
spread: Clumping rosette; self-sows modestly on bare or lean ground
seasons: Basal rosette of crinkled, often purple-flushed leaves through spring; slender spires of purple, rose, or white saucer flowers from late spring into summer; spent seed spikes persist into fall
wildlife: Bees, hoverflies, and butterflies work the open saucer flowers; an RHS Plants for Pollinators selection
deer: Resistant — fuzzy, slightly bitter leaves deter browsing
toxic: Not toxic to people or pets; leaf hairs can mildly irritate skin on contact

care.hardiness: Hardy to USDA zones 4–8, making it fully perennial across the Front Range (Boulder is 5b–6a). Individual plants are short-lived — typically flowering strongly for 2–3 seasons — but they self-sow reliably on lean bare ground, so the colony persists. Once a patch is established, allow a few spent spikes to release seed before cutting them; the seedlings are small first-year rosettes that flower the second year.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant container stock in spring (mid-May after last frost) or early fall (late August–mid-September) into sharply drained, lean soil — on a slope, in a raised bed, or in gravel/decomposed-granite ground. Boulder's heavy alkaline clay is workable only where drainage is excellent; amend with coarse grit or plant on a well-drained berm. In containers, use a lean cactus mix in a terracotta pot; container roots are less cold-hardy than in-ground roots, so move into an unheated garage or shelter over winter. Never site in a low spot that pools water — crown rot is the main killer on the Front Range.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun — at least six hours of direct sun — for erect, well-branched spires. It tolerates light afternoon shade but does best with morning and midday sun; too much shade produces floppy stems.[1][2]
care.soil: Demands sharply drained, lean soil. Rich or heavy clay soil causes weak, sprawling growth and crown rot. It thrives in chalk, loam, sandy or gravelly ground at pH 5.5–8.0 — our Front Range alkaline conditions are a good fit as long as drainage is adequate. Never enrich the planting pocket with compost; lean and sharp is the rule.[1][2]
care.water: Low; drought-tolerant once established. In lean well-drained ground it rarely needs supplemental water beyond the first season. Overwatering — especially in clay — is the primary failure mode. Water new transplants weekly through their first summer to establish, then back off to only the driest stretches.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space plants 12–18 in apart; the basal rosette typically reaches 12–18 in across and the flower spike rises 2–3 ft. Thin self-sown seedlings to this spacing in spring.[1]
care.propagation: Seed is the easiest method — no cold stratification is required; surface-sow on a lean, gritty mix in spring or fall, pressing lightly to good contact (tiny seed needs light to germinate). Seedlings bloom their second year. Allow a few spent spikes to shed seed in place so the colony renews itself. Root cuttings taken in winter (pencil-thick root sections, 2–3 in long, set barely under a gritty medium) are the way to propagate a specific named cultivar or color form true-to-type; spring division works but disturbs the taproot-like crown and is less reliable. Do not divide mature flowering plants.[1][2]
care.sow: Surface-sow on bare, lean soil or a gritty seed mix — press to make soil contact but do not cover, as the fine seed needs light. Sow in spring or late summer; self-sown seed falls naturally in summer and germinates with fall rains.[1]
care.bloom: Late spring into summer, roughly May–July on the Front Range — the slender spires open from the base upward over several weeks. Deadhead spent spikes by cutting them to the base to prolong the bloom season and limit self-sowing to a manageable level. Leave a spike or two to mature and shed seed for colony renewal.[1][2]
care.feeding: None needed; fertilizer produces weak, floppy growth and shortens the plant's life. Leave the soil lean.[1]
care.maintenance: Low. Cut spent spikes to the base after bloom. The evergreen-to-semi-evergreen basal rosette is attractive through winter and can be left in place; tidy any frost-tattered outer leaves in early spring. Watch drainage: if the crown sits wet through winter, it will rot.[1][2]
care.selfsow: Self-sows modestly on bare, lean ground — enough to renew the colony from year to year, not so aggressively as to be weedy. Deadhead consistently to control seeding; mulching bare soil near the plants also suppresses seedlings. Not invasive in Colorado, but self-sowing keeps it present without replanting.[1]
care.troubles: Crown rot in wet, poorly drained, or clay soil is the leading problem — drainage is everything. Powdery mildew can appear in humid or crowded conditions; improve air circulation and avoid overhead watering. Caterpillars (the mullein moth, Cucullia verbasci, and figwort weevil larvae) occasionally skeletonize the foliage; pick by hand or remove affected leaves. Spider mites are possible in hot, dry conditions. Few other pests bother it.[1][2]
care.companions: Pairs naturally with other xeric sun-lovers in a dry garden or pollinator border: bearded iris, salvia (meadow sage), prairie coneflower, knautia, and ornamental grasses. Its vertical spike reads well next to mounding low plants like dianthus, cushion spurge, or prairie zinnia. For a cottage-garden effect, combine with pasque flower (early season), then salvia and knautia as it blooms. Plant toward the back of a bed where the basal rosette can anchor the planting without the spent spike becoming conspicuous.

edible.summary: Purple mullein is a garden ornamental with no documented culinary use — grown for its flowers, not as food.[1][2]
edible.caution: Verbascum phoeniceum has no recognized food use and should not be eaten. The genus Verbascum has a long folk-medicinal history (the common mullein V. thapsus was used as an expectorant herb), but that is V. thapsus, not this species, and it was a medicinal application under specific preparation, never a food. The leaf and stem hairs of V. phoeniceum can cause mild skin irritation on contact in sensitive individuals. It is not classified as a significant poison, but it is not food — treat it as an ornamental only.[1][2]
